misconceptionSummary: Students drop the placeholder zeros after rounding, changing the magnitude of the answer.

steps.narration: The student correctly identified three and eight as the two significant figures, but wrote thirty-eight instead of thirty-eight thousand. Dropping the zeros changed the number from thirty-eight thousand to thirty-eight — a thousand times smaller.

visuals.ruleText: After rounding an integer, replace non-significant digits with zeros — do not drop them
visuals.subText: The zeros hold the columns so the number stays the right size

steps.narration: For integers, the digits you round away must be replaced with zeros. They are no longer precise — but they still tell you the order of magnitude. Without them the answer is in completely the wrong ballpark.
Round down — eight stays as eight. Replace the two and the two trailing zeros with zeros. The answer is thirty-eight thousand, not thirty-eight.

visuals.label: Round the 2nd sig fig → replace remaining digits with zeros

steps.narration: Thirty-eight thousand. Two significant figures — three and eight — with three zeros holding the place value intact.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: writing 38 instead of 38 000 when rounding to 2 s.f. The placeholder zeros must stay — they keep the number the right size.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Never drop placeholder zeros from an integer when rounding. Replace the rounded digits with zeros — they hold the magnitude even though they are not significant.
